mnemonics: B V RAMANA: Binary operation, Verification of axioms, Ring examples, Abelian groups, Morphisms (homomorphisms), Normal subgroups, Applications
keyPoints: A group is a set with a binary operation that is closed, associative, has an identity element, and every element has an inverse.
An Abelian (or commutative) group is a group where the operation is commutative (a*b = b*a for all elements).
A subgroup is a subset of a group that itself forms a group under the same operation.
commonMistakes: Assuming all groups are commutative (Abelian); many important groups, like permutation groups, are not.
Forgetting to check closure when testing if a subset is a subgroup.
Confusing the identity element (e.g., using 1 for additive groups where the identity is actually 0).
